title: Whittled oak marking gauge
subtitle: A simple wooden marking gauge for joinery layout
excerpt: A single-fence marking gauge made from seasoned oak with a sliding beam and a set screw. Used to score parallel lines for joinery work. A useful shop tool that teaches precise whittling and basic tool fitting.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for marking-gauge construction and use. Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry for the fence-and-beam design and the wedge or screw locking method.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried wood at a stable moisture content (8 to 12 percent for indoor use). Dimensionally stable; proportions will not move.
The slot a saw cut leaves in the wood.
A wooden fence sliding on a beam with a steel pin or wheel, used to score a parallel line for joinery layout.

content.text: A
marking gauge
is a small tool with a long history in joinery shops. It scores a line parallel to an edge so the maker can cut precisely to that line. Making one teaches precise whittling on
seasoned wood
and basic tool fitting. Oak is the traditional material: hard enough to resist wear, stable in a workshop environment.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Parts and materials

The gauge has two parts: the beam (a strip 25 cm long and 12 mm square) and the fence (a block 8 cm long, 5 cm wide, and 25 mm thick). You also need one steel pin to act as the scribing point. A headless nail ground to a point works; a panel pin works. The fence slides on the beam and is locked with a wooden wedge or a small set screw through the side of the fence.

Use straight-grained seasoned oak for both pieces. The beam must be straight; if it is twisted even slightly, the gauge will mark inconsistently. Check the beam against a flat surface before starting.
Making the beam

Saw the beam to 25 cm and check the ends are square with the try square. Use push cuts to remove any twist and to chamfer the long edges slightly; this makes the beam easier to slide through the fence. The finished beam should be 12 mm square and straight.

At one end of the beam, bore a hole with a bradawl or a fine bit and drive in the steel pin. The pin should protrude 1.5 mm from the lower face of the beam. This is the scribing point that marks the line on the wood.
Making the fence

Saw the fence block to 8 cm long. Mark a mortise through the middle: 14 mm wide (slightly more than the beam width) and centred on the thickness. Bore a series of holes within the mortise outline with a brace and bit, then clean up the walls with a chisel or with careful slicing cuts using the sloyd knife.
The
kerf

from the tenon saw cut defines the mortise shoulders. Test-fit the beam through the fence: it should slide smoothly with no play in any direction. If the mortise is tight, pare a little from the walls; if it is loose, add a thin wooden shim.
Locking mechanism

The simplest lock is a wooden wedge in a saw-cut on the top of the fence. Cut a 15 mm slot down through the top of the fence to intersect the beam mortise. Whittle a tapered wedge from a scrap of oak or beech. Driving the wedge down into the slot locks the beam by squeezing the mortise walls.
Using the gauge

Set the fence the required distance from the scribing pin and lock it. Hold the fence against the reference edge of the workpiece with the beam running along the face. Draw the gauge toward you along the wood; the pin scores a fine line at the set distance from the edge. The line is ready for a chisel, saw, or knife to follow.
Finishing
The gauge does not need finishing for workshop use. A single coat of Danish oil seals the oak and makes it easier to clean.
Variations
Double-pin mortise gauge: fit two pins in the beam, adjustable apart from each other. Marks both shoulders of a mortise in one stroke.
Wheel gauge: replace the steel pin with a small steel wheel. Cuts a cleaner line on cross-grain.

rows.symptom: Gauge marks a slightly wavy line
rows.cause: Beam is not straight, or the fence mortise has play
rows.fix: Check the beam with a straight edge. Plane or pare any twist. Check the mortise fit; add a thin card shim if there is lateral play.

rows.symptom: Wedge does not lock the beam firmly
rows.cause: Slot in the fence does not reach the mortise, or wedge is not tapered enough
rows.fix: Deepen the slot with the tenon saw. Re-taper the wedge at a steeper angle if it slips back under pressure.
